Data: The students are volunteering a lot and are very eager to share what the recall in the story. They are also eager to dance and share the space with their classmates. - Arleigh
The kids seem excited for each lesson!
kids remembered a lot from the previous week and were eager to share what they recalled.
The kids are continuing to show enthusiasm and interest in each lesson
There was definitely a lot of joy in the room!

Each week I see more and more progress. The kids are remembering, exploring, and building! In the last class of the day, our student Irvin asked if we were going to get to do the activity from last week, and his eagerness was a great reflection on the impact of the language scaffolds!
There are consistently positive responses from the students.
Students are responding very well, the vast majority was engaged, enjoying, showing understanding
The students remember the content of the first visit. They seemed to connect with the topic very well and were engaged with the story, the movement and the music.
She (the teacher) shared she noticed engagement in students who normally are disengaged.
What was successful was the engagement, the recall from the previous lesson, the joy of moving with intentionality and in connection to the music,
All students are participating! The material feels very accesible to them.
(The students) were excited about making the building shapes with a partner and traveling around them. Being a leader is a role that most like.
High participation rate, high engagement, increased verbal and body expression, more independence, more understanding in terms of the connections between the dance and the story
All students engaged in the drawing and writing on the worksheets
Each week the children are making more and more connections. They are doing a great job with building on ideas/creating variations and remembering what we did the week(s) before.

The students are mostly understanding how to build on previous words such as Chop, Shake, and Stir, and they are utilizing those words in creative ways. They are also remembering actions including Leap, Skip, Gallop, and Walk. The idea of Freeze and Travel was very impactful, and the students are also using their knowledge of levels

Ms. Rosa's class had some significant connections and questions this week. One of the kids asked "How do I move like an apple?", and we talked about the different ways we could think about that. Someone asked "How do I move like a blueberry?", "How do we move like a marshmallow". We talked about if the apple was in the tree or on the ground....if the marshmallow was fresh or stale, etc.!
A teacher showed the digraphs SH and CH as we were chopping and shaking to reinforce phonemic awareness

the diverse demonstration of understanding of the action words, the connections with academic learning for example sequence words, vocabulary building, phonemic awareness, logical understanding of a sequence, speaking in full sentences, the active audience noticing similarities and differences between the performers' ideas.

Students seemed to enjoy and understand the connection between the movement and the story and the illustrations, how a story and images can inspire dance, how a prop can represent the aroma and even desks can represent buildings.

I heard a student in the self contained special ed class make a spontaneous sentence about the differences between two narrow shapes he saw others made, a direct application of learning from the previous class.
They were able to make the connection with the story between the aroma and traveling in the curvy pathway

The warmup was also very good because the students were reading the body parts and embodying the fluid quality independently. Students were very ready to explore locomotor actions along the straight pathways before me prompting them.

What was most successful or effective and engaging was the use of the scarves and the concept them representing food and the connection between the movement sentence : meet, dance, part with the story

No matter what the content of the lesson is, to keep the integrity of the dancing I feel I need to highlight the connection to the music and the feeling with it and the theme and stimulate expressiveness with commitment.
understanding of the vocabulary meet and part and the logical sequence of the movement sentence, connection to the feeling of the music
Pointing out the different ways students showed around was helpful to deepen the understanding of the word AROUND.

The worksheet, even though it focuses on the dance, it allows students to express understandings of the story that are not necessarily embodied in the dance but are the imaginary context of the dance.

kids remembered a lot from the previous week and were eager to share what they recalled. It was also evident that they were able to create more variations for their Movement Sentences. The kids seem excited for each lesson!

One of my favorite moments was when a first grader recalled the movement they used last week with the streamer. Although they did not have a streamer to use this time, they were able to tap into that recollectiona

The students already have an increased understanding of the DEL Dance Framework terminology. They successfully explored creating a movement sentence with variation, and identifying similarities and differences. Body, Space, Effort and Relationship were present in each of the classes!

The students are mostly understanding how to build on previous words such as Chop, Shake, and Stir, and they are utilizing those words in creative ways. They are also remembering actions including Leap, Skip, Gallop, and Walk. The idea of Freeze and Travel was very impactful, and the students are also using their knowledge of levels

It was wonderful to be back at the school after February break! Some of the kids volunteered to write the words Meet, Dance, and Part which was very exciting! They also remembered some of our dance vocabulary such as Skip, Gallop, Chop, Walk, Narrow, Wide, and Curvy!
Also, some of the kids were able to write the words for our Movement Sentence "Meet, Dance, Part"!
Some of the classes were able to do an emotions exercise and implement adding emotion to our dancing!

One class also did their three movement sentences in their groups along with transitions. One class worked on their three movement sentences but did not get to the transitions yet. I split the classes into three groups, so each small group is doing a different movement sentence, and then we all come together to do the three movement sentences as a group!

Exploration for the students is continuing and growing which is exciting! The kids are also continuing to associate patterns, understand improved spatial awareness in small groups, enhancing their vocabulary, and thinking about varied ways they can move!
We used inference to identify what's next in the sequence of the movement sentence
Finding similarities and differences is quite essential for dancers. It conveys the message that dancing in the same way as others and in different ways, are all ok, these are choices.
Students came up with different ways to show the shapes concepts in different levels or with different details.

I feel it’s important to check their understanding repeatedly because movement can be very exciting and they may loose the connection with the story. Making connections is helpful. Critical thinking questions too.

Students recall of previous class, students seem to be more aware of their body parts and comfort level with the fluid movement of the warm-up. They were able to demonstrates the 3 types of shapes in the 3 levels. Their spacial awareness as they traveled around the shapes was good.

The warmup was also very good because the students were reading the body parts and embodying the fluid quality independently. Students were very ready to explore locomotor actions along the straight pathways before me prompting them.
I noticed how some students could be very strong in their dance abilities but not so much in their writing, or viceversa.
Repetition. Questions that prompt details to extend the length of the sentence. The use of gestures and movements to elicit verbal language
Dance Scaffolds
demonstrating the movements were very impactful.

I find that visuals and examples of the movements are working, as well as finding things in the rooms that the children can connect to visually. I use a lot of examples based on what is in the space (doorway, window, staircase, tape on the floor, etc)
We also played "Where the wind blows" using our two straight lines and entering the space in the middle when their food/letter/name was called!

4.) Ms. Polanco's Class: We were able to have everyone stand in a circle, hold their scarf in their left hand, and then hold their neighbor's scarf in their right hand (while the neighbor is also holding the scarf), making a connected circle with everyone holding scarves, and then moving our feet apart (part), and then together (meet), traveling to the right slowly then to the left slowly 5.) Students stood in 2 straight lines and did "meet, trade scarves and dance, part", then we added layers like "meet with one leap, dance and trade scarves with your partner, part with walking" 5A.) Students stood in one straight line and then travelled towards me with their gallop, leap, walk, or created shapes that were wide, narrow, round, etc.
5D.) Ms. Sullivan's Class we used carpet squares to meet, dance, part.

3.) "When the wind blows" When I call the first letter of your name/the first letter of your food/the food you get to meet in the middle of the circle, dance, then part when the next group comes in. "When the wind blows DONUTS.....When the wind blows EGGPLANT....." We made a name and food stew with the game "When the wind blows"
I showed twisting my shirt to explain twisted shape
Describing the different responses they produce to the same concept is key to develop independence, creativity and deepen knowledge.
Using the movement sentence : Shape+ in place action + travel action and display it as a visual organizer is effective too.
Question in advance and giving example is effective : telling dancers I will ask them about what's the same and different about the dancers' moves.

I used the following accommodations to include students that were not responding in movement: I had a student play the drum to accompany the movement sentence, and another one pointing to the movement sentence to guide students, and another one hold the cards, and another one make a building shape connected with a teacher for the group to make the curvy pathways around.
identification and demonstration of straight pathway, traveling actions, reinforcement of space words
Some classes needed more prompting and guidance to perform the movement sentence and others were more independent.

Repetition and scaffolding is helping students learn as they apply language learned in previous lessons and in other contexts, or words from the book in their verbal production as well as locomotor movements learned in the third movement sentence were incorporated in the second movement sentence.
Pre-Teaching the Vocabulary Words was very helpful
as well as the vocabulary cards and showing the movements. The pages in the book are also very helpful
1.) Everyone says their name, the first letter of their name, a food they like that starts with the same first letter (and classmates and teachers could help think of the foods)
2.) Warm-Up: Move like the aroma and the broth. Wave your scarf ABOVE your head, UNDER your leg, put your scarf on the floor and walk AROUND your scarf, put your scarf BEHIND your back.
5B.) For Ms. Monegros' class we built a road on the ground with the sentence strips so we knew were to Meet our partner!
This visit was another clear example of the scaffolds that are working and the information the students are retaining

The worksheet for the Thank You note presented some interesting elements for each class. Only one of the classes said they have tried to write a letter/thank you note, so my lesson included explaining writing a salutation, including a closing, etc.

The visibility of the word cards and demonstrations are very helpful. Sometimes I ask one of the teachers or the students to demonstrate with me when I am explaining something. I think these visuals help a lot of the students.

In each class different students have gravitated towards the repetition of "Thick Red Stew, In a Big Fat Pot", or the visual cues for the "Chop + Shake + Stir", or the the idea of the Building and the Aroma, etc.
Repetition is key for each lesson. The visual cues from the cards and writing the sentences on the chart paper have been really helpful.

Students are benefiting from the vocabulary cards, the modeling, chunking, repetition, making connections for example with the word travel like from home to school or swing like the swing in the park, using synonyms like statue for freeze. Seeing the words and the picture helps them say them correctly and then hear them correctly

I invited the ENL provider to come observe a class. She came and said she noticed the use of repetition, visuals, modeling, saying the words, speaking to respond to questions, saying whole sentences, written words.

As the dancers were dancing I named simmilarities and differences I saw, or asked them to identify them, so that they could have some ideas to refer to later when it was time to share the differences and similarities to enter in the Ven Diagram. I felt I needed to scaffold and model this process of compare and contrast.
I heard some read this story last year. The topic is very familiar to them.

I feel it’s important to check their understanding repeatedly because movement can be very exciting and they may loose the connection with the story. Making connections is helpful. Critical thinking questions too.
I used oral sentence frames to help the class make long detailed sentences that connect movement with the story

A good engaging reflection prompt was about being leader and follower. Supporting students make longer sentences by prompting them to add details individually or collaboratively seems and then the whole class repeating them seems effective.
I am working on offering showing and pointing as alternative ways to respond aside from sentences.

Oral sentence frames are great as one student starts a response and I prompt another one to continue the sentence adding more details and I add more information leaving a blank for them to fill in with another detail.

Students are responding very well to the visuals, the written words, repetition, realia, modeling, making connections. Making connections to self: what food are you bringing to Omu's house if your scarf is green?. Inference: why is the pot empty? How does Omu feel? Students were thinking solutions like: Omu could go to the supermarket and buy more ingredients to make more stew, or if the illustration is green it can be salad.
Using the visuals of the book as supports for them to answer questions
Finding synonyms is a great support: students came up with the word separate as an equal to part
I added one that we can call gestures as a was modeling meet, dance, part with my hands after I saw a student use TPR to show meet and part.
the more mature students I allowed them or encouraged them to choose more words

Repetition and scaffolding is helping students learn as they apply language learned in previous lessons and in other contexts, or words from the book in their verbal production as well as locomotor movements learned in the third movement sentence were incorporated in the second movement sentence.

Giving the task for the audience to respond in a full sentence strating with I saw...... and then collaboratively making the sentence longer by prompting students to answer the details to several questions words is a great strategy

I noticed that describing students work using the targeted vocabulary and rich descriptive words can come back to the teacher as in this example : I commented to a student that clearly showed a calm feeling as he was moving in the warm-up " The music feels relaxing to you, right?. At the end of class, he approached me saying: " Dancing with the scarves was relaxing"
Students benefitted from seeing the vocabulary words: the cards and additional words I wrote on the board like scarf, aroma, stew and buildings
The drawing can sometimes express understandings that can't be expressed in writing or even orally
Repetition. Questions that prompt details to extend the length of the sentence. The use of gestures and movements to elicit verbal language
During the same class one of the kids volunteered to write the word STEW, and I found that throughout the day, more of the kids were saying the word STEW more comfortably
They are becoming increasingly articulate with the vocabulary, and they demonstrated great progress with recognizing and articulating words.

The students are mostly understanding how to build on previous words such as Chop, Shake, and Stir, and they are utilizing those words in creative ways. They are also remembering actions including Leap, Skip, Gallop, and Walk. The idea of Freeze and Travel was very impactful, and the students are also using their knowledge of levels

It was wonderful to be back at the school after February break! Some of the kids volunteered to write the words Meet, Dance, and Part which was very exciting! They also remembered some of our dance vocabulary such as Skip, Gallop, Chop, Walk, Narrow, Wide, and Curvy!

Accommodation with understanding that the lesson was sometimes more impactful with having the teacher repeat (in Spanish) what I said, and Amplification with giving the students additional ways to see and practice the vocabulary by taking turns (if they wanted) writing the words on the chart paper!
1A.) Ms. Polanco's Class: some of the kids were able to write their first name and their food (we start in the classroom as kids arrive for first period, then we move to the dance performance space)
1B.) Students in Ms. Monegro's class volunteered to write our movement sentence on our sentence strip. Dylan wrote "Dance" correctly and Emilio wrote "Part" correctly.
We talked about if the apple was in the tree or on the ground....if the marshmallow was fresh or stale, etc.!
enhancing their vocabulary,
We had to distinguish stew from soup.
The material feels very accesible to them. They are using words like cut and mix and then adding the new words chop and stir as synonyms.

understanding of the vocabulary meet and part and the logical sequence of the movement sentence, connection to the feeling of the music

I noticed that describing students work using the targeted vocabulary and rich descriptive words can come back to the teacher as in this example : I commented to a student that clearly showed a calm feeling as he was moving in the warm-up " The music feels relaxing to you, right?. At the end of class, he approached me saying: " Dancing with the scarves was relaxing"
A student looked up the word "watermelon" in a folder he had in his bag in order to use it in his sentence about chopping.

One of the most rewarding parts of today was when I new student was able to say the first letter of her name, "D". The teacher told me that this child was thus far unable to say her name or the first letter of her name due to a speech impediment. (This session was my first time working with the student.) I was doing an exercise with the kids where we said our name, the first letter of our name, and our favorite food that starts with the same letter. When it was this child's turn, the teacher informed me she wouldn't be able to say the "D". The child next to her also has a name that starts with the letter "D", so I repeated the first child's name and that her name starts with a "D", and then I said "also, D" referring to the child. She repeated back to me "also D". It felt like a huge success!
The students are making an effort to try sounding out new words and are excited to participate.

Two of the classes spent the majority of the class writing their name at the bottom of the page, completing their first sentence, and drawing their pictures. We worked on completing the first sentence to guide the understanding of what the kids were planning to draw!
One student asked: I wonder if Omu is going to have enough for herself if she gives stew to everybody
students increased understanding of the story ( people bringing different foods and not stew) ,
There was a lot of success with the kids writing their names or spelling their names, and reading or repeating the movement sentences

I had a case in which a student responded physically to a question, then in Spanish and last in English. So his thinking was transferring from one language to the next.

Using a little Spanish like the word aroma since it’s spelled the same way, is validating and supportive.
Challenge: One of the bilingual classes needed more explanation to understand that people followed the aroma to ask for the stew.
Because I use some Spanish and encouraged students to use words from their language and culture, today I heard "guineo" and "caldito" as food items
A few could express their reflection verbally
I invited students to turn and talk, share with a partner next to them what food they were bringing to Omu's house in reference to the color of the scarf.
Some had to say the sentence out loud before being able to write it. Some needed prompting to bridge from the drawing to creating a sentence
There was only one class that was able to complete the sequence sentences below the drawing.

One of the teachers asked if it was ok if she reads the entire book to the class before our next lesson. She feels that seeing the "bigger picture" and understanding the story is very helpful for her class and helps their interest level. I think this is a great idea, and would love to know if I can go ahead and read the whole book to the class before continuing with the lessons, so they have that "big picture view" of the story!
teachers seemed pleasantly surprised by the words they were able to recognize and say!
I was only able to see 3 of the students from Ms. Cabrera's class. Ms. Cabrera was absent so her students were split up into a variety of other Kindergarten classes.

Accommodation with understanding that the lesson was sometimes more impactful with having the teacher repeat (in Spanish) what I said, and Amplification with giving the students additional ways to see and practice the vocabulary by taking turns (if they wanted) writing the words on the chart paper!

One of the most rewarding parts of today was when I new student was able to say the first letter of her name, "D". The teacher told me that this child was thus far unable to say her name or the first letter of her name due to a speech impediment. (This session was my first time working with the student.) I was doing an exercise with the kids where we said our name, the first letter of our name, and our favorite food that starts with the same letter. When it was this child's turn, the teacher informed me she wouldn't be able to say the "D". The child next to her also has a name that starts with the letter "D", so I repeated the first child's name and that her name starts with a "D", and then I said "also, D" referring to the child. She repeated back to me "also D". It felt like a huge success!

Another one of my favorite parts of the day was when the kids were dancing like marshmallows. :-) The teacher looked up and said "are they supposed to be doing that?", and I said "yea, they are marshmallows!!!". It made her smile and I think she clicked into understanding a little bit more about our program. That also felt like a great win!
I suggested that teachers repeat this movement sentence structure with other vocabulary cards.
Challenge: If teachers choose students to respond to share what they saw, most time they always pick the same students because they are ready to share faster.
A teacher showed the digraphs SH and CH as we were chopping and shaking to reinforce phonemic awareness
Many teachers tend not to use wait-time when they call students to respond. And I hear a lot " use your words". So I notice how these scaffolds are not that common
I notice that the cards and the worksheet on a slide are a great tool to involve teachers in holding cards and writing students choices for their movement sentences.

I invited teachers to read some pages in preparation for this session. Some did and some didn’t .if they don’t, it’s not a problem. It’s just helpful especially for students that need more repetition.

The challenge is the difficulties in spelling and their writing ability and even formulating ideas in a sentence. The starter: It shows, proved effective. I didn't know how much to help them with the spelling. I didn't want the spelling to be on the way of their expression but I also didn't want to contradict the teachers' efforts to encourage students to sound out words

Spontaneously a teacher told me that she appreciated the words exposure that this program provides and another one also on her own initiative shared that she liked the way it was structured. The first one together with a student showed me the word gallop on a book they are reading in class because this student remembered during reading time that we had explored this word in dance class.
. For the most part teachers also got involved with me in supporting students' language expression.
was based on pacing and movement for the class, and one was based on having the shorter period at the end of the day. (Note: Time)

Due to time and "information overload", I shifted how much/which part of the book we read. I started from the beginning of the book again since we only read from it the first week, and I read until the section with the Hot Dog Vendor! (Note: Time)

I reused the worksheets so the kids could identify what they say happening in the room. In one class, I had them circle the actions they used (Wide, Narrow, Round, Leap, Curvy, Gallop, Walk). I asked kids to identify words on the Chart Paper
Yes, I paired down the goals for the day, and did not start the written work, so that the students could stay focused on the movement and movement sentences.

Yes, I wrote out an abbreviated version of what my lesson plan would be, based on what we have covered the previous two-weeks, and then I adjusted according to each class. This is a condensed look at my lesson plan (which also had variation in itself): LESSON OVERVIEW: In Lesson 8 Week 9 Students practice and perform Movement Sentences with transitions - one dance to an audience. Students reflect on their dance experience by writing a thank you note to Oge Mora! Tools Word Cards Chop, Shake, Stir (written) Curvy, Leap, Walk, Skip, Gallop Wide, Narrow High, Middle, Low Round Straight Jump Above, Below, In Front, Behind, Under, Forward, Backward, Around Meet, Dance, Part Supplies Worksheets Pencils Crayons Spots Scarves Chart Paper Blue Tape Warm-Up (5 Minutes) Head to Toe Stew - Broth Chop - Food for the Stew Shake - Salt and Pepper Exploration (13 Minutes) Three Groups - Three Sentences Sentence - Transition - Sentence - Transition - Sentence Ending Section Together Community Stew! Take a Bow!

For the warmup, I am leading it more precisely with the expectation that they copy me moving body parts in an order from top to bottom with the idea we are water filling in the pot. I feel it's more focusing to do it this way for the beginning of a lesson.

Due to lack of time, I had to skip the sharing in two groups in the last period, or I had to skip the development so I had the development /culmination and sharing altogether, or I only had time for only 2 students in each class to reflect in a sentence.
I did less exploring than what the lesson includes but kept the fundamentals. The reason is to keep the integrity of the work with the space and time constraints we have in the classrooms.
In one very small class, I used props to pretend they were buildings to practice the curvy pathways around them.
The vocabulary word aroma and contact could be added to the slide.

For the lack of time, I merged some sections of the lesson like the warm-up game and the exploration. I didn't determine what locomotor movements and what position words for them to use. I went straight to the culmination. I didn't have time for the worksheet

I think it can be more effective to introduce the movement sentence meet-dance-part in one lesson and then develop it further in the next one, and once they are more familiar with the vocabulary, incorporate the worksheet.

Not really, interspersing focusing, self-regulation activities in between sections of the lesson, may be needed at times, but even though they are not written in the lesson plans, they are an intrinsic part of management.

I chunked part of the information during the traveling aroma and the buildings as I had one pair show the traveling aroma in a curvy figure eight around 2 building shapes and then added the other aroma pair. To accomodate time to allow the aroma to change leaders I added the possibility for the buildings to change to another shape in a quiet way so I don't even notice it. Once the curvy figure 8 was clear, then I added the change of locomotor movement.
The challenge to learn the word stew instead of soup made me realize that an image that shows soup would be helpful to see the difference.
I dedicated the whole lesson to work on the worksheet because the timing on the original plan didn't seem realistic.

All of the students have explored the three movement sentences. For the culminating dance, I split the classes into 3 groups, before they merge into 1 big group. For some of the classes, we have rotated the groups so they are taking turns in the different groups (each group is one of three movement sentences). In one class we have reached the part of the dance where the whole group comes together to do all three movement sentences, using space in all areas of our "stage". The students have also selected the transitions from one sentence to the next
were able to take turns being leaders and followers
Another one reflected: My partner and I were kind because we didn't push each other.
Asking for consent for contact with a partner needs to be built in the facilitation

There is a big interest in video games in the classroom, and when one of the kids mentioned Mario (Mario brothers), I used this example for most of the classes. I also used music from Mario Brothers which the kids really enjoyed.

There are a couple things that I think are noteworthy for ideas in the curriculum......for example, the worksheet has the option to put a check mark or X. The Kindergarten classes have not learned how to do a checkmark, but they understand circling something. I also adjusted the lesson so we were only looking for things we noticed rather than "x"ing out options....that way we can still find them later!

I found it helpful to get the kids into 4 or 5 lines with constant dance movement, while establishing the idea of a leader for each line. I then had every child come to the front of the room to lead one warm-up step (from our vocabulary....Round, Wide, Narrow, etc.).
One of the classes also drew pictures of themselves on the worksheet, and I think that was a great way to make connections!

I focused on talking about community, pot luck dinners, connections with food, the great things we each add to every setting we are in, and the ways we can share dance and energy! Regarding the lesson plan..... 1.) I asked the kids what they remembered from the book (who had recently come to Omu for the stew?), 2.) Finished reading the book 3.) Had the kids point out something they noticed on the thank you card page of the book 4.) Talked about community and our special qualities 5.) Had volunteers (in some classes) write Meet, Dance, Part 6.) Made 2 Straight Lines 7.) Meet - say hi to your partner, then Dance!, then Part 8.) Talked about the word part and used the example of a part in our hair! 9.) Meet (come together), Dance, Part 10.) Asked who remembered our vocabulary from 2 weeks ago 11.) Used 2 words - Walk and Meet, Dance and Freeze, Walk and Part, etc. 12.) Cool Down using Wide, Narrow, Round, Breathing in the imaginary aroma of the stew!
One of the students who did not want to participate did a great job assisting me and selected word cards and demonstrated actions for the group!
I wrote the 3 movement sentences, then matched the kids' names to the group they were in, and matched the scarf to the sentence and names!
the kids' names with a Movement Sentence is building an additional connection for them!
making connections for example with the word travel like from home to school or swing like the swing in the park,
This lesson is an introductory one that is not in the curriculum. I didn’t make any significant adjustments except that one student made the shape connected to his paraprofessional’s.
I heard they are using the term “ verbs “ so I will use it too
One girl asked me if I could change the music for the movement sentence, and I did.
I used a salsa music to be culturally responsive and noticed dancing more fully physically and emotionally due to that connection. Only one student did not engage
I added art forms as I saw students saying "hi" to each other as we were practicing meet, this made it more concrete for them.
the worksheet helped students hold on to the group choices
I respond to students' choices by trying them out and naming them. It's the reverse strategy from modeling

There are a couple things that I think are noteworthy for ideas in the curriculum......for example, the worksheet has the option to put a check mark or X. The Kindergarten classes have not learned how to do a checkmark, but they understand circling something. I also adjusted the lesson so we were only looking for things we noticed rather than "x"ing out options....that way we can still find them later!

I think it is challenging to introduce the ideas of going around a partner, while also doing Freeze and Travel, and potentially introducing the idea of "weight bearing partner work" for this age group all in one lesson (Note: Find Lesson)
(I) did make adjustments for each class, but I am finding that the Lesson plans are mostly suited towards the 2nd Grade Classes as opposed to the Kindergarten and 1st Grade Classes.

The lines on the page were hard for the kids to identify. I would love to see more space between the lines and the box for the drawing if possible. Perhaps the Kindergarten classes could make folded cards where the front can say "Thank You" and they can draw a front cover picture during one lesson. Then, on the second lesson, they can write Dear Ms. Mora, a fill in the blank sentence, and sign their name!

next. Regarding Challenges, I want to provide the students with opportunities to work together in smaller groups, while also keeping all of the groups engaged. When I am going over what's happening in one group, sometimes I am asking all three groups to participate in the first sentence so they are all doing the same thing but in 3 areas. Other times I am trying to create smaller tasks so they can stay focused on their one sentence, but I would like to improve on keeping them engaged while they are waiting for instructions
The challenge mainly was due to lack of time
The challenge was in a few cases the self control to freeze after traveling.
I didn’t have enough time to combine the two groups to simultaneously perform their movement sentences.
One of the visuals for the duets showed a shape with a partner in balance, and not connecting hands, which triggered some ideas for shapes that students couldn't hold still.

Yes, eventhough the main goal and content was the same, I couldn't have pairs collaborate to choose one of the traveling movements by planning it, and I couldn't overlap the 2 movement sentences. The reason was: safety in the space, difficulty mastering the curvy pathways.

Challenge: keep the locomotor movement when we did the movement sentence with the scarves in the circle formation. Many just walked to the meet until I prompted them to keep choices that were not walking.
Challenge: One of the bilingual classes needed more explanation to understand that people followed the aroma to ask for the stew.
